reasoning: The most recent data point for the target series on April 11 is 5.75%, which will carry over to become the threshold value on April 13. This 5.75% reading is a significant spike from previous weeks (which were around 5.30%) and represents a near 52-week high. Yields surged in early April largely due to announcements of new tariffs and subsequent inflation fears, but such rapid spikes often experience at least a partial mean reversion in the short to medium term. The FRED baseline statistical tool estimates a 47% probability of closing above 5.75% by April 20. I anchor slightly below this baseline estimate because successful auctions have already begun easing upward pressure on Treasury benchmarks. Given the high starting threshold, the probability of an increase rests below 50%. However, as the forecast horizon extends into the summer and fall, the growing macroeconomic unpredictability—especially regarding Fed monetary policy and global trade tariffs—significantly increases outcome variance. Consequently, in line with the principle that uncertainty structurally increases over longer intervals, the probabilities smoothly converge toward the maximum-uncertainty point of 0.50 for the most distant resolution dates.
